stimulus.content: A person is accused of a crime. At the trial, the person does not speak. The judge tells the jury that the Fifth Amendment gives the accused the right to remain silent.

stem: How does the Fifth Amendment help a person accused of a crime?

choices.text: It lets them pick their own judge
It stops them from being put in jail
It says they do not have to speak against themselves
It gives them a lawyer free of charge

answer_explanation: The Fifth Amendment protects people from being forced to testify against themselves. This is the right to remain silent. Picking a judge, avoiding jail, and free lawyers are not Fifth Amendment protections. The right to a lawyer comes from the Sixth Amendment.
